title: Human capital risk and portfolio choices: Evidence from university admission discontinuities
abstract: Theory suggests that increasing idiosyncratic, uninsurable labor income risk may cause individuals to reduce the risk in their financial assets. This relationship is confounded empirically by the tendency of risk tolerant people to choose riskier careers and hold riskier portfolios, leading to an upward-biased estimate of the effect of earnings risk on risky assets holdings. We overcome this identification problem by exploiting a discontinuity built into the Danish national university admissions system, which provides quasi-random assignment of similar applicants to programs with different earnings volatility profiles. Our methodology allows us to measure the causal impact of enrolling in a high-volatility program, holding fixed the average program earnings and human capital betas. We show that entering a program whose enrollees subsequently experience volatile earnings causes students to have more volatile earnings and, ceteris paribus, to hold fewer risky assets and be less likely to participate in the stock market. We calibrate our empirical results to a portfolio choice model with risky labor income that fits our empirical findings well with modest participation costs, myopic behavior, and reasonable levels of risk aversion.

author.Philippe d'Astous*: Department of Finance, HEC Montréal, 3000 Chemin de la Côte-Sainte-Catherine, H3T 2A7, Montréal, Québec, Canada
author.Stephen H. Shore: Maurice R. Greenberg School of Risk Science, Robinson College of Business, Georgia State University, 35 Broad Street, GA 30303, Atlanta, GA, USA

article.1. Introduction: Human capital risk — specifically labor income risk — is arguably the most important risk people face. When income risk is independent of asset returns and cannot be traded away or diversified, consumers with standard preferences should optimally tilt their investment portfolio away from risky assets (Kimball, 1993; Gollier and Pratt, 1996; Viceira, 2001; Cocco et al., 2005). As a response to this “background risk”, households with higher labor income risk should therefore be less likely to participate in the stock market, and conditional on participation should hold less risky assets.1 Quantifying the effect of earnings risk on financial choices has important implications for asset pricing (Bodie et al., 1992; Danthine and Donaldson, 2002; Addoum et al., 2019), the hedging of labor income through financial markets (Massa and Simonov, 2006; Betermier et al., 2012), and for the link between stock market participation and the equity premium (Vissing-Jørgensen, 2002).However, individuals jointly choose their careers and investment portfolios for reasons driven by common factors that are frequently unobservable to researchers, such as risk preferences. This can lead to an omitted variable bias, causing an attenuation of the effect of labor earnings risk on portfolio risk (Fagereng et al., 2018). Put simply, risk tolerant individuals tend to choose riskier careers and riskier portfolio holdings, so the observed correlation between earnings and portfolio risk will be larger than implied by the true causal effect of income risk on portfolio choices. Furthermore, in order to credibly isolate the impact of earnings risk, researchers must not only use variation orthogonal to unobserved preferences, but also control for other features of the earnings process (level, growth, and comovement with stock returns) that might impact stock holding.To overcome this identification problem, we exploit an institutional feature of the Danish university admissions system that creates quasi-random variation in admission to university programs, and therefore in earnings risk profiles. Prospective university students rank up to eight programs in preferred order and compete for limited enrollment capacity in selective programs based on a composite score that reflects their grades in high school. A central agency allocates placement in the different programs in preferred order to students with the highest scores, giving rise to time-varying program-level admission cutoffs. For students with a score that is close to the cutoff for their more-preferred “reach” program but well above the cutoff for their next most preferred or “safety” program, the admission process effectively randomizes them into programs with different subsequent labor income risk profiles. Students with scores just above the “reach” program cutoff are assigned to that program, while nearly identical students with scores just below this cutoff are assigned to their “safety” program.Our empirical design exploits this exogenous variation in program enrollment for students with a reach-safety program pair, combined with the fact that different programs of study lead students to subsequently experience different amounts of earnings volatility. We build on d'Astous and Shore (2021), who use the same education discontinuities to show that enrolling in a program in which the enrollees have higher permanent earnings volatility increases an individual's subsequent permanent earnings volatility one-to-one, but with no substantive effect on human capital betas.2 These results imply that cross-program variation in permanent earnings volatility reflects causal effects, and can be interpreted as a change in background risk. In this paper, our identification strategy compares students on each side of their admission cutoffs – who will consequently be assigned to high- or low-volatility university programs, respectively – to estimate the effect of increasing earnings volatility on investment, holding fixed the earnings level and human capital beta of the program of study.We find that higher earnings volatility caused by university programs of study leads ceteris paribus to lower stock market participation and, conditional on participation, to a smaller proportion of risky assets in the investment portfolio.3 We show that a one-standard-deviation increase in cross-program permanent earnings volatility (i.e., an increase from 11% to 14% annually) lowers stock market participation by 23% (a 6.5 percentage-point drop from 0.28 to 0.215). Furthermore, conditional on individuals participating in the stock market, a one-standard-deviation increase in cross-program permanent earnings volatility decreases the share of risky assets in the portfolio by 8% (a 2 percentage-point drop from 0.25 to 0.23).Our identification strategy allows us to exploit the rich cross-section variation in programs' earnings process and individual characteristics. We investigate how our estimates vary when we explore heterogeneity in individual characteristics. In particular, models predict that the impact of exogenous labor income risk on portfolio choices will vary with financial wealth, human capital wealth, and age (Campbell and Viceira, 2002). We show that individuals with higher labor earnings have larger background risk effects. Because the importance of financial wealth naturally increases relative to human capital wealth over the life cycle, extrapolating from these findings would predict smaller background risk effects for older workers.We also investigate other potential mechanisms that could be at play. First, the program-level share of stock market participation could be driving our results, either through program peer effects (e.g. Brunello et al., 2010; Feld and Zölitz, 2017; Booij et al., 2017) or knowledge about the stock market (e.g. Hong et al., 2004; Ouimet and Tate, 2020). We rule out this channel by simultaneously instrumenting for the share of individuals participating in the stock market in the program of study. Next, we investigate the possibility that low-risk programs of study allow students to accumulate wealth faster, causing the increase in stock market participation. We augment our specifications by simultaneously instrumenting for financial wealth and find that our conclusions are unchanged. Finally, program ranking by prospective students or other reach and safety program features could affect our results through characteristics that correlate with permanent earnings volatility. To rule out this possibility, we include reach and safety program fixed effects to our estimations, in order to capture any fixed characteristics of the programs that could be affecting our results. Again, we find that this channel cannot explain our findings.While earnings volatility – driven by variation in program of study – causes people to take less portfolio risk, the data show no raw association between earnings volatility and portfolio risk. This divergence between correlations and causal effects can be explained by risk-based selection into programs of study. Students inclined to hold risky portfolios tend to select into programs of study with volatile earnings, even though those programs cause them to take less portfolio risk. To illustrate this, we decompose our results in terms of average earnings and permanent earnings volatility from one of the least risky broad fields (education) to one of the riskiest fields (business). We show that the null association found in the data could be the result of an omitted variable bias, highlighting the need for an identification strategy to measure the causal effect of earnings risk.We use our IV estimates to derive target moments to which we calibrate a single-period model of portfolio choice with risky exogenous labor supply, following the setup in Campbell and Viceira (2002). Our model targets the average risky asset share in our sample and its IV-derived gradient with respect to labor earnings volatility, as evaluated at average earnings. By comparing our empirical estimates with the calibrated model results, our model can rationalize our empirical findings with modest participation costs (averaging 390DKK, $55), myopic behavior (consumers consider 1.1 years of future labor earnings), and reasonable levels of risk aversion (coefficient of relative risk aversion of 12).We would expect background risk effects stemming from risky labor earnings to be more important for individuals with higher labor earnings (holding wealth fixed). This qualitative prediction is borne out in our model calibration and empirical estimates, both of which show the gradient of the risky asset share gets steeper as the level of earnings increases. This variation with respect to earnings provides additional untargeted moments that provide an out-of-sample test of our calibrated model. While point estimates from our IV regressions suggest a somewhat larger role than the model does for the level of earnings to increase background risk effects, the model's predictions are well within the confidence intervals implied by our regressions.1.1. Contribution to the literatureSimilar admission discontinuities to those we exploit here have been used in other contexts, most often to measure the effect of program of study on earnings.4 While we use these same discontinuities, our work differs by studying how individuals adjust their portfolio choices in response to features of their program of study, notably its permanent earnings volatility.Beginning with Guiso et al. (1996), a growing literature has documented the effect of labor income risk on household investment portfolios. Some evidence suggests that labor income risk typically has small effects in deterring stock market participation, or the fraction of risky assets held in investment portfolios (Fagereng et al., 2018).5 Bertaut and Haliassos (1997) study long-run precautionary motives in portfolio allocations and show that it is persistent risk that affects rebalancing, rather than short-run risk. This idea is put forth more directly by Angerer and Lam (2009) who study the association between earnings risk and portfolio choices by explicitly separating permanent and transitory volatility.However, unobservable risk preferences could correlate with both career and portfolio choices; the effect of risk preferences on portfolio allocations was established some decades ago (Cass and Stiglitz, 1972; Cohn et al., 1975),6 and research suggests that risk preferences also affect educational and career choices (e.g. Saks and Shore, 2005; Nielsen and Vissing-Jorgensen, 2006; Calvet et al., 2021). While the literature has recognized that the correlation between earnings risk and portfolio choices is likely to suffer from an omitted variable bias as a result, an issue remains because of the difficulty in finding sources of exogenous variation in earnings risk.Fagereng et al. (2018) discuss how these selection issues, notably unobserved risk aversion, can bias estimates of the effect of labor income risk on portfolio choices. To overcome these issues, the authors exploit variation over time in firms' profitability risk. The authors find that when the profitability of a worker's firm becomes more volatile, workers hold fewer risky assets. Their findings imply large background risk effects even though large changes in the volatility of firm profitability have little impact on the volatility of labor income, since firms insulate workers from most of the change in firm profitability risk.Our paper shares with Fagereng et al. (2018) a common concern that unobserved risk aversion can bias estimates of the effect of labor income risk on portfolio choices. Whereas their paper highlights the role of firms in insuring their workers against changes in profitability risk, we find that moving from a low-volatility to a high-volatility University program of study has a large and roughly one-for-one impact on an individual's permanent earnings volatility. As a consequence, we explore an empirical setting with substantial variation in the labor income risk that individuals face and against which they are not insulated. This allows us to explore the impact of uninsurable changes in labor income risk over a wide range of risk values.Betermier et al. (2012) analyze the portfolio holdings of households who switch industries between 1999 and 2002, in order to evaluate the effect of earnings risk on portfolio choices while holding individual preferences constant. There is evidence that firms differ in the earnings volatility of their employees (e.g. as correlated with technological innovation Kogan et al., 2020), and the paper tests the degree to which this association is causal. They find effects of switching across industries but no level effects, suggesting that differences in cross-sectional preferences influence both earnings volatility and portfolio choices.7 Jo (2020) studies the effect of state-level changes in employment protection laws using the Survey of Income and Program Participation, and finds an increase in stock market participation and the share of their financial wealth held in risky assets. Finally, Calvet and Sodini (2014) find a smaller role for labor income risk in the portfolio holdings of identical twins using twin fixed effect regressions as identification strategy, under the assumption that twins have the same risk preferences.8Compared to previous studies, the rich cross-sectional variation in program earnings allows us to credibly study the causal effect on financial decision-making of several features of the earnings process simultaneously and investigate heterogeneous effects. This is an important feature of our empirical design because programs may differ along other dimensions in addition to earning volatility, such as their level, growth, and human capital betas. Our results show that these other earnings process features do not mediate the causal impact of earnings volatility on portfolio choices. To our knowledge, this paper is unique in cleanly estimating the causal impact on portfolio choices of all of these earnings process features simultaneously.Finally, our results suggest that quasi-random changes in university program of study pass through to portfolio choices because of exogenous changes in earnings risk. Previous studies have identified that a welfare cost may exist due to the imperfect matching between prospective students' risk preferences and the earnings risk associated with their program of study (Jensen and Shore, 2015; Barth et al., 2017). We contribute to this literature by showing that students use education to sort into occupations that differ in their earnings risk, based on their risk aversion. We also provide evidence that individuals adjust their financial behavior in response to the effect of earnings volatility stemming from their program of study.
article.2. Institutional setting: 2.1. Danish national university applicationOur study design is based on the institutional details of university admissions in Denmark. Prospective students apply to university through a central agency that allocates students to programs with limited enrollment capacity, which creates plausibly exogenous variation in the probability of acceptance into different programs. As described in Andersen et al. (2020) and Heinesen (2018), university admission in Denmark is based on prospective students' high school performance. A standardized score ranging from 0 to 13 in 0.1 increments is calculated based on students' grades in their senior years of high school, taking into account the level and difficulty of the classes. Students can list up to eight programs (that is, university-major pairs such as International Finance at Copenhagen Business School) in order of preference. Admission is granted to programs in preferred order to students with the highest scores until all seats are filled. For competitive programs, this generates an admission cutoff, according to which students with a score above the cutoff are admitted, while those with a score below it are not.Admission to most programs depends only on the prospective student's high school score, with two exceptions. First, admissions can be made through two different “Quota” systems. The Quota 1 admission system, with applications in June, is intended for students applying directly after completion of high school. These prospective students are evaluated for admission into their chosen programs solely based on their composite high school score. The Quota 2 admission system, with applications in March, also evaluates prospective students based on their composite score, but allocates additional bonus points for extra-curricular activities. This system is usually chosen by students with relevant work experience for the programs to which they are applying. In our data we cannot observe these bonus points, thus the admission rule for Quota 2 partially depends on unobservable factors. In the sample we analyze, 54.4% of applications are made through the Quota 1 admission system and the remainder through Quota 2.Second, when applying to different programs, students have the option to be included on a standby list in case they are very close to the cutoff for their preferred program but are not accepted (typically, in such cases they are 0.1 to 0.2 points below the admission cutoff). If any students drop out of the program in the first few weeks of the semester, applicants on the standby list will be offered places in the program. If no students drop out of the program, applicants on the standby list are guaranteed admission to the program in the following year. In our sample, about 40% of students choose this option when applying. Because these applications are conceptually different and allow students to be admitted to programs despite having a lower score than the admission cutoff, we rule them out in our analysis.The institutional setting of university admission in Denmark is ideal for studying the effect of education on individuals. Because the system credibly leads to exogenous variation in admission to different programs, it de facto randomizes students who are close to the admission cutoffs into programs with different earnings processes. The fact that randomness dictates admission to different programs for students close to the admission cutoff allows us to control for the potentially endogenous self-selection into university programs. If risk-averse students tend to enroll in programs for which earnings are less volatile, studying the relationship between earnings volatility and stock market participation could lead to erroneous conclusions regarding its effect. Instead, as described below, we focus on marginal students who are either slightly under or slightly above the cutoff for their preferred program, such that a small change in the admission cutoff would lead to admission to different programs.2.2. Admission discontinuitiesWe restrict our focus to the sample of students who choose a viable pair of “reach” and “safety” programs. The students included in this sample are those for whom the admission system generates quasi-random assignment to different programs. To be eligible for our sample, students had to be within 0.5 points over or under the admission cutoff for their preferred reach program and at least 0.5 points above the admission cutoff for their less-preferred safety program. This ensures that small changes in admission cutoffs due to annual supply and demand will generate discontinuous variation in the enrollment program of these students. When the admission system is implemented strictly based on a student's high school score, admission to either their reach or safety program is determined according to the following equation:(1)pi={ri if Di≥0si if Di<0, where pi denotes the enrollment program of student i, ri and si denote their reach and safety programs, respectively, and Di represents the distance between the student's score and the admission cutoff for their reach program. However, as detailed in Section 2.1, a number of complications arise such that the admission system does not operate exactly as detailed in equation (1). First, Quota 2 applications are noisier than Quota 1 applications because we are not able to observe the bonus points assigned to students based on their extra-curricular activity. This means that some students could effectively be closer to (over or under) the admission cutoff of their reach program than what we observe. Second, because some programs require the completion of specific classes for admission, another factor we are unable to observe, admission could be denied despite the student having a score that exceeds the admission threshold. Third, for students whose scores fall exactly at the admission cutoff for their reach program, an algorithm breaks ties. We do not have details on these tie-breakers, but we verified that exclusion of such students does not alter the conclusions of our study.Fig. 1 shows the empirical admission discontinuities for students applying via the Quota 1 and 2 admission systems. Panel (a) shows that for Quota 1 applications, as students cross the admission cutoff for their reach program from below, their admission probability jumps discontinuously to quickly reach about 90%. At the same time, their admission probability for the safety program drops from about 75% to less than 10%. This shows that students slightly to the right of the admission cutoff for their reach program are discontinuously more likely to enroll in their reach program, while students slightly to the left of the same admission cutoff are discontinuously more likely to enroll in their safety program. This provides the source of variation we exploit in our analysis.9 Panel (b) shows the same information for students who apply via Quota 2. We can again see discontinuous differences in the probabilities of enrolling in reach or safety programs around the admission cutoff of the reach program, although the discontinuity is smaller than in Quota 1. This additional noise comes from the fact that students in the Quota 2 admission system may get additional bonus points, which we do not observe. For analysis, we pool applications from both admission systems but include Quota fixed effects and measure the probability of acceptance in the reach program separately to effectively treat both types of application independently.Download: Download high-res image (530KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. We pool all programs and years and present the distance between the application score and the admission cutoff for the reach program on the x-axis. We use the sample of 10,567 students who have a reach–safety programs pair in application years 1994 to 2006. Panel (a) shows the subsample of applications made through the Quota 1 system of admission (6,339 observations) and Panel (b) shows Quota 2 applications (4,228 observations).2.2.1. Validity of the admission discontinuitiesThe Danish National University admission system closely relates to serial dictatorship, which is both Pareto efficient and strategy-proof (Svensson, 1999), subject to limits on the number of programs that can be ranked. This generates credible variation in admission to different university programs (Kirkeboen et al., 2016). However, a potential problem could arise if prospective students were able to guess the cutoff of their preferred programs based on public information about past cutoffs and manipulate their rankings to increase their chances of entering their preferred program. In this section, we present year-to-year variation in cutoffs, as well as formal density manipulation tests.Panels (a) and (b) of Fig. A.1 show how cutoffs move from year to year, both unconditionally and based on the distance between the student's current score and the previous year's admission cutoff.10 This figure shows that there is significant year-to-year variation +/- 1 point around admission cutoffs, which also justifies our choice of estimation bandwidth around the cutoffs. In our main results, we also control for the previous year's admission cutoffs, so that the results are identified from changes in the admission cutoff. Panel (c) presents the density of applications around the cutoffs. There is no evidence of bunching around the cutoffs, however we formally test this in the next section.Table A.1 presents the p-values for density manipulation tests developed by Cattaneo et al. (2019) under different assumptions. The estimation details are presented in the table's notes. Only 1 of these 12 cases (less than 10%), fails to reject manipulation of the assignment variable at the 5% significance level. This provides evidence that the admission system works and that individuals are quasi-randomly assigned to different university programs based on their scores. We further show that average individual characteristics are also smooth around the cutoffs, and present the results of the first-stage estimations for our main model in Sections 4.6 and 5.2, respectively.
article.3. Models and estimation: 3.1. Earnings process modelWe follow Meghir and Pistaferri (2004) in specifying the earnings process as(2)wit=ct+β′Xitw+uit, where wit is the logarithm of real annual earnings,11 ct is a calendar year effect, and Xitw is a vector of observable characteristics.12 uit represents the unexplained (or excess) component of earnings which can be decomposed into a transitory innovation and a martingale permanent component such that(3a)uit=τit+πit, and,(3b)πit=gi+πit−1+ζit, with τit representing the transitory shocks to earnings, and πit the permanent components of earnings.13 The excess component of earnings growth is defined as Δuit and total volatility is defined as (Δuit)2≡σ2. We define the variance of the transitory shock τ as στ2, and normalize it by presenting its additive inverse. We define the variance of the permanent shock as σπ2.3.2. Earnings process estimationThe moment conditions that identify the permanent and transitory shocks are(4a)−E[ΔuitΔuit−1]=E(τit2)≡στ2, and(4b)E[Δuit(∑j=−(1+q)(1+q)Δuit+j)]=E(ζit2)≡σπ2. In the analysis, we replace uit with the residuals of the regression in equation (2). We replace the theoretical moments with their sample analog and denote them with hats, such as σˆπ,i2 and σˆτ,i2 for the estimated individual-level permanent and transitory volatility, respectively. We then average the various attributes of the earnings process by program of study p to create program-level averages. We denote these averages with overlines such as σ‾π,pi2 and σ‾τ,pi2 for the average permanent and transitory volatility of the student's enrollment program pi, respectively. We set q=1, in which case the transitory shock follows an MA(1) process, and the permanent shock is a composite MA(2) process.143.3. Effect of earnings volatility on individuals' financial holdingsOur main equation of interest relates stock holdings to earnings process attributes:(5)SHi=Γ(yi or y‾pi)+βXi+μi, where SHi represents measures of stock holdings such as a binary variable indicating stock market participation, and a continuous variable indicating the fraction of risky assets held in the investment portfolio, y‾pi and yi represent vectors of earnings process attributes, measured either at the program- or individual-level, and defined in Section 3.1 (e.g., excess earnings u, earnings growth Δu, total volatility σ2, transitory volatility στ2, or permanent volatility σπ2), Xi represents a vector of control variables, and μi is the error term.15 In our main findings, when y‾pi represents the permanent volatility of earnings, Γ measures the extent to which program-level permanent earnings volatility affects stock market participation or the share of risky assets in the individual's portfolio.16 In Section 5.2.2, we investigate the effect of individual-level earnings process attributes and find qualitatively similar, albeit less precise, results.3.4. Effect of program of study on individuals' earnings processAs a first step in our analysis, we investigate the extent to which average earnings from programs of study affect individuals' earnings processes. We build on the results in d'Astous and Shore (2021) who use the same econometric model to predict the various individual-level outcomes yi based on the average attributes y‾pi of the program of enrollment:(6)yi=δy‾pi+βXi+ϵi, where, again, yi represents a vector of possible outcomes from the various attributes of the earnings process, y‾pi denotes the analogous program-level average outcome of the individuals who enroll in the same program pi as individual i, Xi denotes individual covariates, and ϵi is the error term. When y‾pi represents the permanent volatility of earnings, δ measures the extent to which the permanent volatility of program earnings affect an individual's own permanent earnings volatility.3.5. Fuzzy regression discontinuity designDirectly estimating equations (5) and (6) may not reveal an unbiased estimate of Γ and δ in the presence of selection problems, that is, if cov(yi,μi)≠0 or cov(yi,ϵi)≠0. For example, if risk-averse individuals tend to select both fields of study in which earnings are less volatile and less risky investment portfolios, a classic omitted variable problem will plague the estimation of the causal effect of earnings risk on financial holdings, causing an attenuation bias. To overcome this identification problem, our analysis uses the admission discontinuity presented in Section 2.2 as a source of exogenous variation in admission to programs of study.In both equations (5) and (6), we obtain causal estimates of Γ and δ by restricting our estimation sample to students near the admission cutoff of their preferred reach program, and using admission discontinuities to estimate a fuzzy regression discontinuity IV design. The first-stage regression used in our fuzzy regression discontinuity design is given by:(7)yi or y‾pi=ϕXi+χZi+ξi, and the following variables are included as instruments in Zi:(8)P(pi=ri|Di)×y‾ri; and P(pi=si|Di)×y‾si, where y‾ri and y‾si denote the various attributes of the earnings process, averaged for the reach and safety programs, respectively. For example, we instrument permanent volatility using P(pi=ri|Di)×σ‾π,ri2 and P(pi=si|Di)×σ‾π,si2. The first-stage equation uses the source of variation in our instruments Zi to identify the effect of changes in admissions on the future earnings processes of admitted students. These instruments are good candidates to use in a regression discontinuity design because they capture the variation in the earnings process attributes across reach and safety programs and account for selection in the admission process by interacting these with the probability of being admitted to the reach and safety programs. This is a valid identification strategy because, when comparing scores just below and just above the reach program cutoff, the probability of being admitted to the reach and safety programs discontinuously increases and decreases, respectively.To test the validity of our instruments, Fig. A.2 provides a graphical representation of the first-stage equations (7), by replacing the continuous measure of the probability of enrollment in the reach program with a sequence of dummy variables reflecting distance from the admission cutoff. Each student has exactly one such dummy variable, equal to 1, representing their own distance from the admission cutoff, while all other dummies are equal to 0. This figure shows that all first-stage outcomes have large discontinuous jumps around the admission cutoff, confirming that as students cross the admission discontinuity from below, they are exposed to the earnings process of their reach program instead of the safety. These jumps are large, discontinuous and are estimated very precisely, such that concerns about weak instruments are absent in our context.17 To further verify the validity of these instruments, we present bunching and other density manipulation tests in Section 2.2.1. The results show no evidence of manipulation around the admission cutoff.In the analysis, we use the predicted values from equation (7) in two-stage estimation models, for which the second stage equations are represented by equations (6) and (5). When using more than one attribute of the earnings process in our estimation, we instrument each attribute using its analogous set of equations (8). We use reach and safety averages to instrument both individual- and program-level attributes of the earnings process. Because our analysis shows that permanent earnings volatility is determined one-for-one by program-level earnings volatility, our main results use program-level earnings volatility in equation (5), and we demonstrate that our results hold using individual-level earnings volatility, although they are less precisely estimated.
article.4. Data and descriptive statistics: Our study uses administrative data available through various Danish agencies, as detailed below. We start by constructing the population data, which consists of the universe of individuals that apply to university for the first time between 1993 and 2006.18 We retain only individuals for whom we have access to the complete set of variables used in our analysis and obtain a total of 321,091 valid student application-year observations. We use this population data to construct program-level averages. We then construct our main sample, consisting of the subset of these applicants who have a reach-safety program pair. From this sample, we obtain 10,567 observations, which we use in our main tests described in Section 3.5.Table 1 presents individual-level descriptive statistics for the main variables used in the analysis. We present averages for the entire population of students applying to university for the first time (all applications; left), as well as the subsample of these students who have a reach-safety program pair (used sample; right). This allows us to show how individuals in our reach-safety sample differ from the population in general, and to present the average of program-level attributes as calculated in the population of first-time applicants.Table 1. The number of priorities ranked refers to the number of programs to which the student applied. For the sample of students with a reach–safety pair, we present the average rankings of the reach, safety, and enrollment programs, as well as the distance from the admission cutoff of the reach program, and the share of students for which both the reach and safety programs are in the same field or institution. Panel III presents information on earnings, which are assumed to follow the process described in equation (2). Earnings are averaged 8 to 12 years after enrollment. Present value of human capital wealth is calculated as the present value of lifetime earnings from the age of the individuals to their 64th birthday, using the measure of individual earnings averaged 8 to 12 years after enrollment and a discount rate of 4.1%. The definitions of the different attributes of the earnings process follow from this equation and are averaged over the period 8 to 12 years after enrollment. Panel IV presents information on financial wealth, averaged over the period 8 to 12 years after enrollment. Financial wealth is the sum of stocks bonds and bank deposits, and total wealth is the sum of financial wealth and the present value of human capital wealth.4.1. DemographicsFirst, we obtain the demographic characteristics of individuals up to 2016 through the Danish Civil Registration System. From these data, we obtain a unique identification number for each individual, allowing us to match their records from other registries. Panel I of Table 1 shows that students in both samples are about 21 years old when initially applying, and close to 40% are male. Individuals in our main sample are somewhat positively selected based on the wealth and education characteristics of their parents.4.2. Educational dataSecond, we use admission data from the Ministry of Education on the universe of prospective students applying to university programs. For each individual, these data include their high school score, their program application rankings, the program to which they were admitted (if any), whether their application was submitted via Quota 1 or 2, and whether they opted to be placed on a standby list. The data also include the official cutoff figures for each program. Panel II of Table 1 shows that students in our main sample have higher scores (8.8 vs 8.2) and rank one more program than those in the population. Typically, the rank of their reach program is first and that of their safety is second. We also show that students are on average 0.1 below the admission cutoff for their reach program, and that 60% of them rank reach and safety programs in the same fields, and 30% at the same institution.4.3. Earnings dataWe obtain earnings information for the period of 1993 to 2016 through the Danish Tax Authority. These data are directly reported by employers to the fiscal authority. We bottom code earnings at 100,000 DKK and top code them at the 99% level in the population of first-time university applicants.19 From these data, we estimate the attributes of the earning process for individuals and programs, as described in Section 3.1. We estimate this model in the population data by pooling all individuals between the ages of 25 and 55 during the period of 1993 to 2016.Panel III of Table 1 presents the earnings characteristics of these prospective students. While they all start out with similar initial earnings, students in our main sample take about one more year to complete their program and find a job than students in the general population. The attributes of the earnings process are averaged 8 to 12 years after enrollment. They are similar across both groups, although excess earnings growth and earnings risk are larger in our main sample, while the correlation between earnings and stock market returns is smaller.Panel III also presents the variables we use to proxy for expected human capital wealth. As a simple measure, we first use earnings averaged 8 to 12 years after enrollment. This measure reflects the cross-sectional heterogeneity in expected human capital wealth in our sample, but does not proxy for its total value. Next, we construct the present value of human capital wealth by calculating the present value of lifetime earnings from the age of the individuals to their 64th birthday, using the measure of individual earnings averaged 8 to 12 years after enrollment and a discount rate of 4.1% (as in Catherine et al., forthcoming).4.4. Financial wealth dataLastly, we obtain financial wealth data for the period of 1993 to 2016 through the Danish Tax Authority. These include the amount of savings in bank deposits and the amount invested in stocks and bonds, all of which are directly reported to the fiscal authority by the financial institutions. We categorize as investments in “stocks” any investments in the stock market, which could include exchange traded funds or mutual funds in different asset classes. We winsorize financial data at the 1% and 99% levels in the population of first-time university applicants. From these data, we create a measure of stock market participation as a dummy variable indicating whether the individual holds stocks in any of the years studied, as well as the ratio of total stocks held to total financial wealth as a measure of the riskiness of the investment portfolio. Panel IV of Table 1 shows the financial wealth of both samples. In both cases, 28% of individuals hold stocks and 8% hold bonds. The reach-safety sample is somewhat positively selected, with individuals holding on average 42,000 DKK in stocks (vs 37,000 DKK in the general population), and 38,000 DKK in bonds (vs 35,000 DKK), conditional on holding these financial instruments. Virtually every individual in our main sample holds bank deposits with an average value of 73,000 DKK in our main sample (vs 67,000 DKK).Financial wealth is defined as the sum of stocks bonds and bank deposits, and total wealth as the sum of financial wealth and the present value of human capital wealth. We construct the ratio of the present value of financial wealth to human capital wealth as a proxy for the relative importance of human capital wealth in an individual's total wealth. This ratio varies from 0 to 1. Because our sample consists of relatively young individuals, the expected value of their human capital wealth largely exceeds that of their financial wealth; it is on average 5.39% in our reach-safety sample. Lastly, the unconditional ratio of stocks to total financial wealth is 7% for both samples, reflecting low individual participation in the stock market. Conditional on participation, this ratio increases to 25%.4.5. When relevant, the program-level standard deviation of each variable is presented in parentheses, while the standard deviation across programs is presented in brackets. We present descriptive statistics for the main variables used in our analysis conditional on fields of study in Appendix Table A.2. Although our analysis is based on discontinuities in the admission process at the program level, this table facilitates an understanding of the variation in the data. The table reveals variation in all dimensions of the earnings process across fields. For example, humanities and science majors take the longest to complete their studies, law and engineering have the highest initial earnings, humanities have the highest total volatility, and business and law have the highest permanent volatility. In terms of financial wealth, business programs have the highest rate of stock ownership, and the highest value of stocks held, whereas education has the lowest analogous values. This provides evidence that there is substantial variation in stock market participation across fields, which we exploit in the main analysis. article.5. Empirical results: In this section, we present our main results using the identification strategy detailed in Section 3.5, as well as the analogous non-instrumented results. For interpretation of the results, it is useful to compare the estimates for the IV and OLS equations. The OLS equations, by construction, include causal effects of the earnings process but also self-selection and program effects, whereas the IV results are identified from the exogenous variation in program admission and therefore isolate the causal effect of program-level attributes on individual outcomes. In all our results, we report heteroscedasticity-consistent standard errors.5.1. We instrument each component of the earnings process using the analogous instruments presented in equation (8).21 Panel B shows the results estimated with OLS regressions. In both panels, the dependent variables are individual-level outcomes of the earnings process (e.g., excess earnings growth and volatilities) and the main explanatory variables are the various attributes of the earnings process, measured at the program level.Table 3. First, we demonstrate that excess earnings are caused by programs of study: an additional $1 in excess program-level earnings is estimated to increase individual earnings by $0.53. In contrast, earnings growth is largely driven by self-selection. This can be seen by comparing the IV and OLS estimates; for excess earnings, both estimates are similar and statistically significant, whereas for earnings growth, the OLS estimate of 0.84 is highly significant, while the IV estimate is not. Program-level earnings volatility effectsThe left panel of Table 4 shows that program-level permanent earnings volatility strongly influences stock ownership and the ratio of risky assets to total financial wealth.24 The marginal effect of permanent earnings risk on stock market participation is −6.36, which implies that a one percentage-point increase in permanent earnings risk (roughly one standard deviation of the enrollment cohort average, as documented in Table 2), reduces stock market participation by 23% (a 6.5 percentage-point decrease from a baseline of 28 percent). The marginal effect of permanent earnings risk on the share of risky assets held, conditional on participation, is −1.88, which implies that a one percentage-point increase reduces the risky asset share by 8% (a 2 percentage-point decrease from a baseline of 25 percent). Table A.8 shows that these results are robust to different econometric specifications.Importantly, the non-instrumented analogous results of these equations do not detect any relationship between the volatility of program-level earnings and individual investment behavior. This shows that there is likely to be an important attenuation bias in the non-instrumented estimation of these effects.25 To better understand this null result in raw association, Appendix Table A.6 presents further evidence of financial holding by prospective student who rank riskier programs in their university application. In that table, we reproduce our main findings from Table 4, but additionally present the coefficients on the control variables for the reach and safety earnings volatility controls used in our main specification. Although these variable enter our specification solely as controls, they are informative about the effect of program rankings by prospective students on financial holdings. Specifically, the results show that students who rank programs with higher permanent earnings volatility also are more likely to hold stocks, and conditional on participation, they hold a larger share of their portfolios in risky assets. These effects are as large as the instrumented results we present, which explains why there is a perfect offset of earnings risk characteristics in the raw associations presented in Table 4.5.2.2. Individual-level earnings volatility effectsAn alternative identification strategy uses the same admission discontinuities, as well as reach and safety program attributes, to instrument for individual-level earnings risk. Because of the limited time series of data available, individual-level earnings attributes are noisier, which could lead to less precise estimation results. In the right panel of Table 4, we replace the program-level earnings process attributes used in our main results with their individual-level analogs, using the same reach and safety program instruments as used previously. The results are qualitatively similar to the results using program-level attributes. For stock market participation, permanent earnings volatility is still significant at the 1% level, with a marginal effect of -3.56. Its effect on the share of risky assets is significant at the 10% level, with a similar marginal effect of -2.05. This lower statistical significance and attenuation bias (compared to program-level estimates) is to be expected when measuring noisy individual-level earnings processes, due to limited time series income data.When using individual-level earnings estimates to predict portfolio choices, the first-stage results presented in the right panel of Table A.4 show a Hansen over-identification p-value of 0.80, which again rules out that our instruments might be correlated with the error term. The F-stats range from 2 to 9, which reject the null of weak instruments at the 95% confidence interval, but also suggest that the program-level instruments used for our individual-level estimates are not as strong. We investigate each instrumented variable further by running tests of weak IV robust inference following the work by Olea and Pflueger (2013); Pflueger and Wang (2015), and Finlay et al. (2013) in Table A.5. We find that the admission discontinuity instruments are weaker for individual-level earnings risk than for program-level earnings risk. Although this should caution against using the individual-level estimates, Table 4 nonetheless shows that our main conclusions hold for both the baseline program-level estimates as well as the individual-level ones.5.2.3. Quantifying the effect of earnings volatility on individual financial holdingsTo further illustrate the implications of our findings, we show how our results can be used to explain the gap in stock market participation between business and education majors. In terms of field-specific averages, Table A.2 shows that, compared to education, business has higher excess log earnings (0.091 vs -0.025), higher permanent volatility (0.018 vs 0.006) and a higher stock market participation rate (0.40 vs 0.21). We make use of the marginal effects of excess earnings and permanent earnings volatility to decompose the participation gap between business and education majors into the causal effects of earnings and earnings risk, and residual selection. First, given that permanent earnings volatility is 0.018−0.006=0.012 higher in business than in education, this would lead to a rate of stock market participation that is 7.8 percentage points lower (i.e., (0.018−0.006)×−6.50) in business than it is in education, attributable to permanent earnings risk, ceteris paribus.Second, given that excess earnings are 0.091−(−0.025)=0.116 higher in business, this would lead to a rate of stock market participation that is 6 percentage points higher (i.e., 0.116×0.51=0.06) in business than in education, attributable to excess earnings, ceteris paribus. Therefore, based only on the effects of earnings and their volatility, the stock market participation rate should be 0.078−0.06=0.018 lower in business. However, Table A.2 shows that stock market participation is 0.40−0.21=0.19 higher in business than in education. Out of this gap, −0.078 comes from differences in permanent earnings volatility and 0.06 comes from excess earnings. The excess gap between these two majors is therefore 0.208, and is due to a mix of self-selection and the effect of the program of study. This illustrates the fact that using the association between earnings risk and stock market participation will miss large offsetting effects of the program of study. This is consistent with Panel B of Table 4 which shows no association between permanent earnings risk and financial holdings.Another way of scaling our results is in terms of the share of the variation in portfolio holdings that can be explained by education-induced permanent earnings volatility. First, note that Table 2 shows that the cross-sectional standard deviations of individuals' and programs' stock shares are 0.17 and 0.033, respectively. This implies that (0.033/0.17)2≃3.8% of the variation in an individual's stock shares can be explained by their program of study. Table 2 shows that the cross-sectional standard deviation of programs' permanent volatility is 0.008, and Table 4 shows that the coefficient on the causal impact of permanent volatility on stock share is -2. Therefore, 1% of the variation in individuals' stock shares (i.e., (22×0.0082)/0.172) — and 24% of the variation in programs' stock shares (i.e., (22×0.0082)/0.0332) — can be explained by the causal effect of the program of study's permanent earnings risk on stock shares, respectively.5.3. HeterogeneityTheoretical models with risky labor income, such as Campbell and Viceira (2002), suggest that responses to an increase in labor income risk will vary based on the ratio of financial wealth to total wealth (financial wealth and human capital wealth combined), risk aversion, and age. As the ratio of financial to total wealth increases, the impact of increasing labor income risk on financial risk-taking should become smaller. This implies an in-sample prediction borne out by the data that individuals with relatively more financial wealth should have smaller background risk effects. It also implies an out-of-sample prediction or extrapolation that older individuals – who are largely absent from our identification sample and would have relatively more financial wealth – will have relatively smaller background risk effects.In Table 5, we extend the baseline results of Table 4 by interacting our main program-level earnings attributes with excess earnings (u), financial wealth, the ratio of financial wealth to total wealth, and age. We present the sample average of these characteristics in the last line of the table to ease the interpretation of these results. Columns (1) and (6) of Table 5 repeat the baseline results presented in Table 4, and the other columns present the results with these interactions. Columns (2) and (7) interact our baseline results with a simple measure of human capital: excess earnings (u), averaged 8 to 12 years after enrollment. Our background risk effects are larger for individuals with higher excess earnings. The marginal effect on the interaction of permanent volatility and average earnings is -21.5 for stock market participation and -8.13 for the risky share, conditional on participation. The effects are significant at the 5% and 1% level, respectively, and show that individuals with higher levels of human capital reduce their exposure to risky assets more in response to an increase in permanent earnings volatility, as predicted by theory.26 We use the results from this specification in calibrating the structural model and its out-of-sample predictions in Section 6.Table 5. Columns (4) and (9) interact our baseline results with the share of total wealth that comes from financial wealth.27 Our background risk effects are smaller for individuals with a higher share of financial wealth. The marginal effect on the interaction of permanent volatility and average earnings is 28.6 for stock market participation and 9.06 for the risky share, conditional on participation. These effects are significant at the 10% and 5% confidence level, respectively.28 Finally, columns (5) and (10) interact our baseline results with age. Our results are noisy and not significant, but with the expected sign that background risk effects decline with age as labor income becomes relatively less important. Our noisy results are unsurprising since we have little age variation in our data to exploit.Although our empirical setting presents limited age variation (most individuals apply to university around the same age), our sample offers significant differences in the relative importance of financial and human capital wealth. In line with theoretical predictions, we observe larger background risk effects for individuals with higher labor income and a lower share of financial wealth. We infer from this that background risk effects would likely be smaller for workers older than those in our sample. This is because, as people age, the relative significance of their financial wealth and the present value of their labor income naturally shift, with financial wealth gaining importance over time. Furthermore, earnings volatility typically decreases as individuals enter middle age (Low et al., 2010), leading to reduced labor income risk and, consequently, smaller background risk effects for older workers compared to those in our sample.5.4. Human capital betasWhen earnings comove with stock market returns, human capital betas can be created to measure the relationship (e.g., Guvenen et al., 2017; Addoum et al., 2019). If individuals consider the comovement of their earnings and stock market returns when making portfolio choices, they will tend to shy away from risky assets when their returns comove with their own earnings. We test for this relationship in our data, but find no evidence that programs of study have a causal effect on human capital betas. As such, the admission discontinuities we study do not provide convincing variation in program-level variation in human capital beta, and therefore we do not find that they affect portfolio choices in our sample.We nonetheless present our results in Appendix Table A.7. We first calculate annual program-level comovement between real earnings growth (Δuit) and stock market returns and investigate its effect on individuals' own human capital beta. We use both the Danish OMX C20 and the S&P 500 indices in percentage and log returns, and measure the average correlation 8 to 12 years after application. In Panel I, we show that cross-program variation in human capital betas does not explain individual-level human capital betas.29 In Panel II, we show that instrument program-level human capital betas do not affect stock market participation or the portfolio share of risky assets. Finally, in Section 5.5, we also investigate the impact of controlling for human capital beta when measuring the effect of permanent earnings volatility and find that it does not affect our main findings.5.5. Alternative mechanisms and robustnessIn this section, we investigate alternative mechanisms and econometric specifications for our main results.5.5.1. Alternative specificationsOur identification strategy allows us to instrument for different attributes of the earning process simultaneously, as well as to control for different observable characteristics. We first show robustness to using different specifications for our main results using program- and individual-level earnings risk measures in Table A.8, Table A.9, respectively. We focus our presentation on the results in Table A.8, but all our results are qualitatively similar using individual-level measures, although our statistical power is reduced in certain instances. Each table presents different econometric specifications of our baseline findings in Table 4, reproduced and bolded in columns (3) and (9).In Panel A, we instrument each explanatory variable using their analogous set of equations (8) and in Panel B, we present the non-instrumented results. In columns (1) to (4) and (7) to (10), we vary the set of simultaneous instruments from the program-level earnings process used to estimate our main results on stock market participation and the portfolio share of risky assets. This allows us to verify that other attributes of the earnings process are not driving our results. In columns (5) and (11), we include in the instrumental estimations the same set of controls included in our baseline non-instrumented estimations from Panel B, namely parents' wealth, income, and education, as well as students' grade, age, and gender. This allows to compare the IV and non-IV results with the same control variables. Finally, in columns (6) and (12) we omit the admission probabilities from the previous year from our identification strategy. In all instances, our main findings are not altered by such specification changes.5.5.2. Alternative mechanismsProgram-level stock market participation. Next, we investigate potential alternative mechanisms that could explain our findings. We focus our presentation on the results using program-level measures in Table A.10, and Table A.11 presents the individual-level equivalent. Our baseline findings of Table 4 are reproduced and bolded in columns (1) and (6). We first explicitly consider the probability that the share of individuals participating in the stock market in the program of study could influence our results through peer effects or knowledge of the stock market. To rule out this possibility, we instrument the share of individuals in the student's program that invest in the stock market using our same identification strategy and instruments. Columns (2) and (7) both show that, although the program-level share of individuals that participate in the stock market has an effect on participation and the share of risky assets held, this effect is not sufficient to significantly change our results, which are both still significant at the 1% level.Program-level human capital betas. In Section 5.4, we show that the human capital beta is not strongly caused by programs of study, nor does it predict stock market participation or the share of risky assets in our estimation strategy. To investigate the robustness of our results to their inclusion, we therefore control for the non-instrumented version. In columns (3) and (8) of Table A.10, Table A.11 present econometric specifications that control for human capital beta, and we find that our main findings are still unaffected by such inclusion. This provides novel evidence of the role of earnings risk in portfolio choices, even when holding fix human capital betas.Program-level financial wealth. Next, we consider the possibility that graduates from low-risk programs might accumulate financial wealth faster than their peers in riskier programs, which could drive the rise in their equity share. To rule out this possibility, we instrument financial wealth of individuals in the student's program using our same identification strategy and instruments. Columns (4) and (9) of Table A.10 show that the inclusion of this variable does not alter our findings, so that the level of financial wealth cannot be driving our findings.Other program characteristics. Another potential mechanism that could explain our findings relates to unobserved program characteristics. This would be a potential concern in our analysis if program rankings by prospective students or other aspects of the reach and safety programs correlate with permanent earnings volatility to drive the results. In columns (5) and (10) of Table A.10 we therefore include fixed effects for each prospective student's reach and safety programs, effectively controlling for all constant effects of both programs.30 The effects are 12% to 15% smaller, but are both statistically significant when testing at the 5% level. This provides evidence that other program characteristics cannot explain our main findings. Together, these results therefore suggest that variation of permanent earnings volatility across programs of study is driving the main effect documented here.315.5.3. Alternative earnings process assumptionsNext, we investigate the robustness of our findings to assumptions about the earnings process. Our main results are based on excess earnings measured as the residuals from equation (2), averaged 8 to 12 years after enrollment. In Table A.12 we show the results for program- and individual-level earnings measures, using raw and excess earnings, and alternatively averaging the attributes of the earnings process 8 to 15 years after enrollment. Panels A to D, and Panels E to H show robustness to these alternative assumptions as they relate to our findings in Table 4. Our main conclusions remain unchanged with these different assumptions.Our earnings attributes are estimated over the full sample period to maximize the precision of our estimates. However, one concern about this approach is that earnings attributes potentially contain information on future earnings attributes of the program that workers couldn't observe at the time they apply to University. As an additional test to address this concern, in Table A.13 we re-estimate our program-level variables using only the information available to individuals up to their own cohort-application year. We show that our results are qualitatively unchanged. Because we use fewer data points to calculate our program-level variables in this context, the results are noisier than our baseline results. Nonetheless, our interpretation is unaffected by this change, suggesting limited concerns about using future information in our baseline results.5.5.4. Alternative estimation bandwidthsLastly, we demonstrate the robustness of our estimates for stock market participation and the share of risky assets presented in Table 4 to different bandwidths around admission cutoffs in Fig. 2. article.6. Model calibration: In this section, we calibrate a single-period model of portfolio choice with risky exogenous labor supply, following the setup in Campbell and Viceira (2002).32 The model takes as given from historical data the mean and standard deviation of asset market returns. Standard portfolio choice models typically aim to target investors' observed risky asset share, and we target this moment as well. In our setting, the average risky asset share in our sample is relatively low at 25%. Our identification strategy estimates a second moment that we target in our model calibration, the causal impact of earnings volatility on the risky asset share, evaluated at average earnings. Our model calibration therefore targets these two moments (average risky asset share and its causal gradient with respect to earnings volatility) with two parameters (risk aversion and the time horizon over which agents in the model value their future labor earnings). Additionally, we target the average stock market participation rate (28%) and its gradient with respect to earnings volatility evaluated at average earnings. These moments are targeted with two parameters (the mean and standard deviation of agent's stock market participation costs). The model does a good job of matching these targeted moments with plausible parameters (risk aversion coefficient of 12 and a 1.1 year horizon over which labor earnings are valued).In addition to targeting the average risky asset share (and participation rate) and its causal gradient with respect to earnings volatility evaluated at average earnings, our identification strategy allows us to see how well the model calibration fits additional untargeted moments. These untargeted moments are the same moments as the targeted ones (the risky asset share and its causal gradient with respect to earnings volatility), except that they are evaluated at the 25th and 75th percentiles of the program-level earnings distribution instead of at average earnings. These untargeted moments come from the estimates in columns (2) and (7) of Table 5, which show the causal impact of earnings on the risky asset share (and stock market participation rate) as well as the variation with respect to level of earnings in the causal gradients of the risky share (and stock market participation).33 Our model also does a good job in matching these untargeted moments.6.1. Model setup and optimizationWe follow Campbell and Viceira (2002) and others in solving a single-period portfolio allocation problem in the presence of an independent, exogenous labor income risk. Investors maximize expected utility with a CRRA utility function u(C)=C1−γ−11−γ, defined over consumption C. We consider two independent types of risks: exogenous labor income risk and financial asset risk. Consumption in the final period is therefore given by C=W+Y, which represents the liquidation value of the risky investment portfolio W plus the realization of the risky labor income Y.Investors have initial financial wealth denoted by W0, and can adjust financial risk-taking by allocating wealth between a risky asset and a risk-free asset; the proportion invested in the risky asset is denoted by ω, with the remainder 1−ω invested in the safe asset. The risk-free asset's return Rf is a constant, with rf=log⁡(1+Rf). The risky asset's return R is a random variable that follows a lognormal distribution, with r=log⁡(1+R)∼N(r‾,σr2). The risk premium is denoted by μ≡r‾−rf. The final financial wealth is given by W=W0[ω(1+R)+(1−ω)(1+Rf)]=W0(1+Rp), where Rp is the portfolio return. Labor income Y is a random variable that also follows a lognormal distribution, with y=log⁡(Y)∼N(y‾,σy2).34 We calibrate y‾ to the data by introducing a human capital multiplier, estimated relative to annual earnings. This multiplier is the number of years of future earnings that the agent considers when valuing their labor earnings.The investors' only choice is to select their risky asset share ω, and this decision is made before the outcomes for labor income and the risky asset return are realized. Investors may choose not to participate in the risky asset market by selecting ω=0. Investors are assumed to be unable to sell the risky asset short or borrow to invest in risky assets 0≤ω≤1. We assume that choosing to buy risky assets ω>0 carries a participation cost that may deter some investors from participation (Mulligan and Sala-i Martin, 2000; Vissing-Jørgensen and Attanasio, 2003; Guiso and Jappelli, 2005). Investors who choose to invest any non-zero amount in risky assets incur a participation utility cost (PUC), so that(9)pi={Eu=Eu(C)−PUCifω>0Eu=Eu(C)ifω=0. We assume that the participation utility cost (PUCs) for each investor is known by them, but not by the researcher, and varies from investor-to-investor. We model the certainty equivalent of these PUCs as being normally distributed in the population CE(PUC)∼N(p‾,σp2).35We perform 1,000,000 Monte Carlo draws of each y and r, and use these draws to calculate expected utility as we optimize the model. We optimize to find the value of ω that maximizes expected utility Eu(C|ω) for a given set of assumed model parameters, given these draws for labor income and risky returns.6.2. Target moments and calibration of model parametersTable 6 presents the model inputs, the target moments and the calibrated parameters. We draw our model inputs from two sources. First, we use several moments drawn directly from our reach-safety sample, averaged over the period 8 to 12 years after enrollment: 1) financial wealth (87,848 DKK) and its deciles; 2) annual earnings (282,578DKK/year); and, 3) permanent volatility of log annual earnings (0.015 per year). Second, we use financial market inputs reported in the literature. We set the annual risk-free rate to 3.30% following Nielsen and Risager (2001). We then set the Danish annual equity risk premium to 2.87% and the annual standard deviation of risky asset returns to 19.85%, following the Denmark-specific calculations in Dimson et al. Values in panel B are taken from the literature. Values in panel D are calibrated from the model inputs.The model is calibrated to target the average risky asset participation rate (28 percent), the average risky asset share for those who participate (25 percent), and their causal gradients with respect to earnings volatility, as predicted by our IV estimates and evaluated at average earnings. Specifically, we set the target risky asset share evaluated at sample average wealth, level of earnings, and earnings volatility to be its sample average. We then trace out variation in this target with respect to earnings volatility (still evaluated at sample average wealth and level of earnings) using our IV estimates from Table 5 (evaluated at sample average earnings).36 Since the target moments we trace out are nearly linear with respect to earnings volatility, targeting these moments boils down to targeting two moments: a mean (average risky asset share) and a slope (its gradient with respect to earnings volatility). We do the same for the stock market participation rate.To match the two targeted risky asset share moments, we vary two parameters in our model calibration: risk aversion (γ), and the human capital multiplier showing the number of years over which the agent values future labor earnings (y‾). To match the two stock market participation rate moments, we subsequently vary two other parameters in our model calibration: the mean (p‾) and standard deviation (σp) of the certainty equivalent of participation utility cost.37The calibrated parameters are presented in Panel D of Table 6. We find a risk aversion parameter of 12.35, and a human capital multiplier of 1.10.38 The value we find for risk aversion is in line with the use of a value of 10 in Cocco et al. (2005). It is also close to the values between 8 and 12 reported for a model without cyclical skewness in Catherine (2022), and to the values between 11 and 14 found in Fagereng et al. (2017). The value on the human capital multiplier is in-line with research showing that individuals tend to be myopic with respect to their pensions (see Cremer and Pestieau, 2011, for a review of this literature). Our context is similar in the sense that the uncertainty of labor earnings resolves over a long horizon, which can induce myopic behavior (Himarios, 2000). We find a mean and standard deviation for the certainty equivalent of the participation cost of 389.70 DKK (about $58) and 161.31 DKK ($24). These values are similar to Vissing-Jorgensen (2002), who finds that an annual cost of $50 is enough to explain the choices of 50% of non-participants, and Paiella (2001), who finds that an annual cost of $70 is enough to rationalize the non-participation behavior of individuals.To understand why our model calibration implies that consumers are highly myopic, consider that financial wealth is negligible for most individuals in our sample compared to the present value of their lifetime human capital wealth. Since financial wealth represents only a tiny fraction of total wealth for most consumers in the model, even a very small equity premium is enough to push non-myopic consumers with reasonable risk aversion to hold highly leveraged risky asset positions. Models without myopia would therefore predict counterfactually high risky asset shares and high stock market participation rates. However, individuals in our sample have only a 25% risky share on average. When consumers are myopic, this increases the relative importance of financial wealth as a share of total wealth. Myopia reduces the perceived value of human capital wealth, so it lowers the optimal risky asset share to observed levels (without resorting to extremely high levels of risk aversion that would lead the risky asset share to be counterfactually sensitive to changes in labor earnings risk).Another option to lower the optimal risky share to observed levels would be make consumers pessimistic, for example by introducing a perceived disaster risk that lowers the expected excess return on risky assets to nearly zero (e.g. Fagereng et al., 2017). While this approach could be used to calibrate the model to the observed average risky asset share, it fits the IV-estimated gradients of the risky asset share with respect to human capital risk less well.6.3.
