The moments predicted by our calibrated model are very close to the targets derived from the IV point estimates and are well within the confidence bands.The first and third rows compare calibrated model predictions to untargeted moments evaluated, for low labor earnings (the 25th percentile of the program-level average income, 235,181 DKK) and high labor earnings (the 75th percentile of the program-level average income, 326,130 DKK), respectively.39 Model predictions are well within the 95th percent confidence bands derived from our IV estimates, suggesting that the model fits these untargeted moments as well as the targeted ones.We would expect background risk effects stemming from risky labor earnings to be more important for individuals with higher labor earnings (holding wealth fixed). As a result, we would predict the gradient of the risky asset share (and stock market participation rate) to be steeper for those with higher labor earnings. This qualitative prediction is visible in Fig. 3, where the lines (both estimated in the data and predicted by the model) become steeper as they move from lower to higher labor earnings. The model not only matches this prediction of increased gradient with earning qualitatively, it matches quantitatively as well. However, the gradient becomes steeper with earnings at a somewhat higher rate in the IV estimates than in the model; point estimates from the IV suggest a somewhat larger role for the level of earnings in effecting background risk than the model does, though well within the confidence interval.For our targeted moments evaluated at average earnings, our empirical results imply drop in the risky asset share from 26.9% when labor earnings risk is low (volatility at 0.005) down to 23.2% when labor earnings risk is high (volatility at 0.025). We match this targeted slope of -185% ((23.2%-26.9%)/(0.025-0.005) very well with our calibrated slope of -185%. Our empirical results show that this slope is flatter (-52%) when earnings are low and steeper (-318%) when earnings are high; the risky asset share is more responsive to changes in labor earnings risk for those with more labor earnings. Our calibration matches this pattern qualitatively, but shows a smaller difference in slopes between the low-earning and high-earning cases (-145% and -221%, respectively). Low- and high-earning people in our sample differ more in their responsiveness to labor earnings risk than would be implied by our calibrated model. However, the standard errors in our empirical findings are large enough that we cannot reject the hypothesis that the untargeted calibrated slopes fall within our confidence intervals.
article.7. Conclusion: In this paper, we study the effect of earnings volatility on portfolio choices. Because career and investment choices are jointly determined, we use an identification strategy to isolate exogenous variation in earnings risk. We exploit an institutional feature of the Danish National University system which allows us to study a sample of students who are quasi-randomly assigned to different programs of study that vary in the subsequent earnings profiles of their enrollees. d'Astous and Shore (2021) show that permanent earnings volatility passes through from programs of study to individuals one-for-one. We use this source of variation in earnings volatility to study its effect on financial decisions.We show that individuals with exogenously higher earnings volatility are less likely to hold risky financial assets. This is consistent with the theory of background risk that an exogenous increase in idiosyncratic, uninsurable labor income risk should lead individuals to reduce the risk in their financial portfolios (Kimball, 1993). The effects that we estimate are fairly large. A one-standard-deviation increase in permanent volatility due to the program of study decreases an individual's stock market participation by 23% and, conditional on participation, decreases the share of risky assets held by 8%. However, offsetting effects of excess earnings, self-selection and program of study obscure this relationship in raw correlations. We present evidence that this finding is robust to considering human capital betas.Our causal estimates of the elasticities of portfolio choices with respect to earnings risk provide additional moments that can be used to pin down the underlying parameters of a standard investment problem. This model is able to match untargeted moments exploiting heterogeneity in model estimates with respect to the level of earnings with modest participation costs, myopic behavior, and reasonable levels of risk aversion.
